card.intuition: Pick a pivot, partition everything smaller to its left and larger to its right, then recurse on both halves.
card.analogy: Organizing a messy shelf: pick one book as the reference point, put every shorter book left and every taller book right, then repeat for each pile.
card.time_complexity: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst
when_to_use: General-purpose in-place sorting when average-case performance matters
When cache performance is critical — quicksort has better locality than merge sort
When you need the k-th smallest element (quickselect variant)
gotchas: Worst case O(n²) occurs when pivot is always the min or max — use random pivot selection to avoid
Not stable — equal elements may change relative order
Recursion depth can hit O(n) in the worst case, causing a stack overflow on large sorted inputs

exercise.prompt: Complete the partition function used by quicksort. It should place the pivot in its correct sorted position.
exercise.solution:
arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]  # swap smaller element forward
arr[i + 1], arr[hi] = arr[hi], arr[i + 1]  # place pivot
hints: i tracks the boundary of elements smaller than pivot — swap arr[i] and arr[j] when arr[j] <= pivot
After the loop, the pivot (at arr[hi]) belongs at index i+1 — swap it there
exercise.test_code:
a1 = [7,2,9,4,1,8,3,6,5,10]; e1 = sorted(a1[:])
quicksort(a1, 0, len(a1)-1)
assert a1 == e1, f'Expected {e1}, got {a1}'
a2 = [3,1,2]; quicksort(a2,0,2)
assert a2 == [1,2,3], f'Expected [1,2,3], got {a2}'
a3 = [5]; quicksort(a3,0,0)
assert a3 == [5]
a4 = [2,1]; quicksort(a4,0,1)
assert a4 == [1,2], f'Two-element: {a4}'
print('PASS')
